paper_metadata.title: Combining Human Expertise with Artificial Intelligence: Experimental Evidence from Radiology
paper_metadata.sample: 227 professional radiologists

productivity.finding: On average, AI assistance does not improve the diagnostic performance of radiologists; it helps when the AI is highly confident but harms when the AI is uncertain, and it slightly increases the time taken per case.

inequality.finding: While the AI is more accurate than 78% of radiologists, its assistance does not improve average performance, and a secondary analysis found the effect is not a function of radiologist baseline skill, suggesting it does not systematically compress performance gaps.

substitute_complement.finding: For diagnostic tasks, AI and human radiologists are substitutes, with an optimal delegation policy assigning most cases to either the AI alone or the human alone, but rarely to a human assisted by AI.

findings.comparison: AI assistance vs. no AI assistance
findings.metric_definition: Absolute difference between the radiologist's reported probability and the diagnostic standard. A higher value indicates worse performance.
findings.note: The effect is not statistically significant.

findings.comparison: AI assistance vs. no AI assistance
findings.metric_definition: Time in seconds spent by a radiologist on a case.
findings.note: The effect represents a 3.8% increase in time taken compared to the control mean of 154.3 seconds.

findings.comparison: AI assistance vs. no AI assistance
findings.metric_definition: Absolute difference between the radiologist's reported probability and the AI's prediction.
findings.note: This shows that radiologists do respond to the AI's suggestions, even if it doesn't improve their performance.

findings.metric: Share of human radiologists outperformed by AI
findings.comparison: AI vs. human radiologists in the sample
findings.metric_definition: The percentage of radiologists in the study with a lower Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC) than the AI model.
findings.note: The AI's AUROC was 0.85.

result.one_line: In an experiment with 227 radiologists, a highly accurate diagnostic AI failed to improve average performance because radiologists underweighted its advice and ignored signal correlations, making AI a substitute for human work rather than a complement.
result.qual_notes: This study conducted a randomized controlled trial with 227 professional radiologists to assess the impact of a predictive AI (an image classifier) on diagnostic accuracy. The experiment varied access to AI predictions and patient clinical history. The key finding is that radiologists exhibit 'automation neglect' (underweighting AI) and 'signal dependence neglect' (ignoring correlation between their own signal and the AI's), which explains why a superior AI tool fails to improve, and sometimes harms, average performance. Consequently, the optimal policy is to substitute humans with AI on some tasks, rather than complementing them with AI assistance.

evidence.snippet: Treatments where AI is provided reduce this baseline average deviation by 18.3%.
First, we see that AI with an AUC of 0.85 is more accurate than 78% of humans, which means that a majority of radiologists would do better by simply following the AI prediction.
In total, 227 radiologists participated in our experiment.
Approximately 14% of our participants are US-based
We collaborated with VinMec healthcare system in Vietnam to recruit their staff radiologists.
The term AI is used broadly but in this setting refers to a neural net-based image classifier.
In contrast, AI assistance does not significantly improve average performance.

In secondary analysis of our data conducted after the release of this working paper, Yu et al. (2024) shows that the effect of AI assistance is not a predictable function of radiologist characteristics or baseline performance.

In fact, an optimal delegation policy suggests that cases should either be decided by the AI alone or by the radiologist alone. Only few cases are optimally delegated to radiologists with access to AI. In other words, we find that radiologists should primarily work next to as opposed to with AI.

paper_metadata.title: Artificial Intelligence and Groups: Effects of Attitudes and Discretion on Collaboration

productivity.finding: Team collaboration with AI is highest when teams have positive attitudes and voluntary use is allowed; mandatory use suppresses collaboration in positive teams but elevates it in negative teams.

inequality.finding: Within a team, individuals with more power may direct lower-status members to be the ones to interface with AI systems.

substitute_complement.finding: AI can act as a teammate that complements human work by augmenting decision-making and freeing members from time-consuming tasks, but simple automation can also remove team power and autonomy.

result.one_line: This conceptual paper theorizes that team collaboration with AI is highest when teams have positive attitudes and its use is voluntary, while mandatory use suppresses collaboration in positive teams but can elevate it in negative teams.
result.qual_notes: This is a conceptual paper that develops a theoretical framework for human-AI team collaboration. It proposes that the level of collaboration is determined by the interaction between a team's pre-existing attitudes toward AI (positive vs. negative) and the discretion they have in using it (voluntary vs. mandatory). The model predicts asymmetric effects: mandatory use suppresses collaboration in teams with positive attitudes but can increase it (from a very low baseline) in teams with negative attitudes. The paper does not present empirical data.

evidence.snippet: Katerina Bezrukova¹, Terri L. Griffith², Chester Spell³, Vincent Rice, Jr.¹*, and Huiru Evangeline Yang⁴*

Group & Organization Management
2023, Vol. 48(2) 629-670

Artificial Intelligence and
Groups: Effects of
Attitudes and Discretion
on Collaboration

In this conceptual paper, we identified and assessed research across multiple disciplines to synthesize new knowledge about two factors (attitudes and discretion) that may drive human collaboration with AI on the team level.
Virtual AI display as text (e.g., chatbots like ChatGPT).
We also focus on AI that can choose how to communicate, adapt, and make decisions within the team (Mercado et al., 2016; Myers et al., 2019).

These two aspects guide our creation of a team-level conceptual framework modeling how AI introduced as a mandated addition to the team can have asymmetric effects on collaboration level depending on the team’s attitudes about AI.

When AI is viewed positively by the team, the effect of mandatory use suppresses collaboration in the team. But when a team has negative attitudes toward AI, mandatory use elevates team collaboration.
Individuals in the group with the most power can direct lower-status individuals to interface with the AI.

For example, if a team of employees use AI to augment the team's decision-making process (Leyer & Schneider, 2021), then team members may be freed from time-consuming tasks by the AI and can direct efforts to other areas.
On the other hand, AI that simply automates might remove power from a team accustomed to making decisions through its own processes (e.g., Zuboff, 2019).

paper_metadata.title: Lawyering in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
paper_metadata.sample: 59 law students at the University of Minnesota

productivity.finding: Access to GPT-4 substantially and consistently increased the speed of completing legal tasks by 12-32% but only slightly and inconsistently improved the quality of legal analysis.

inequality.finding: On tasks where AI was beneficial, it disproportionately improved the quality of work for the lowest-skilled participants, compressing the performance distribution.

entry_level.finding: The experiment focused on tasks representative of work performed by junior attorneys, finding that AI can significantly improve their efficiency.

result.one_line: In a randomized trial with law students, GPT-4 assistance substantially increased the speed of completing legal tasks by 12-32% and compressed performance gaps by disproportionately improving the quality of work for lower-skilled students, while average quality gains were small and inconsistent.
result.qual_notes: This randomized controlled trial assigned 59 law students at the University of Minnesota to complete four realistic legal tasks (drafting a complaint, contract, employee handbook section, and client memo) with or without GPT-4 assistance. The study found large and consistent time savings across all tasks but small and inconsistent quality improvements, with some tasks showing no quality gain or even a slight decrease. The primary quality benefit was for lower-performing students, indicating a skill-leveling effect, whereas time savings were uniform across skill levels.

paper_metadata.title: Generative artificial intelligence enhances creativity but reduces the diversity of novel content
paper_metadata.sample: N=293 writers and N=600 evaluators from the Prolific platform in the United Kingdom

productivity.finding: Access to generative AI ideas increases the novelty and usefulness of creative stories by 6-7%, with larger gains from having access to more ideas, but it also reduces the diversity of the stories produced.

inequality.finding: Generative AI disproportionately benefits less creative writers, substantially improving the novelty and usefulness of their stories while having little effect on highly creative writers, thereby compressing the performance gap.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI acts as a creative complement by providing story ideas, but third-party evaluators perceive this as a partial substitution of human effort, attributing 25-31% less ownership to writers who used AI assistance.

result.one_line: In a creative writing experiment, generative AI assistance improves the novelty and usefulness of stories, especially for less-creative individuals, but also reduces the diversity of the content produced.
result.qual_notes: This online experiment (N=293 writers, N=600 evaluators) randomized writers into receiving no AI help, one AI-generated story idea, or up to five ideas. The study finds that while AI boosts creativity metrics, particularly for low-skill writers, it leads to homogenization, with AI-assisted stories being more similar to each other. A key secondary finding is that evaluators attribute significantly less ownership to writers who used AI, suggesting a perceived substitution of human creativity.

evidence.snippet: We find that the provision of Al ideas improves the story's novelty by 6.7% (b=0.259, p=0.001, see Table S3; compared to the Human only mean of 3.85)
and its usefulness by 6.4% (b=0.319, p<0.001; compared to the Human only mean of 5.02).
With respect to novelty, writers in the Human with 1 GenAl idea condition experienced an increase of 5.4% (b=0.207, p=0.021, see Table S4) over writers without GenAl access;
whereas writers in the Human with 5 GenAl ideas condition showed an increase in novelty of 8.1% (b=0.311, p<0.001) over writers without GenAl access.
The usefulness of stories from writers with access to one GenAl idea is 3.7% (b=0.185, p=0.039) higher than that of writers with no GenAl access.
Having access to up to five Al ideas increased usefulness by 9.0% (b=0.453, p<0.001) over those with no GenAl access
For low-DAT writers, having access to 1 GenAl idea improves a story's novelty by 6.3% and having access to 5 GenAl ideas yields improvements of 10.7%.
Among the most inherently creative writers (i.e., high-DAT writers), there is little effect of having access to GenAl ideas on the creativity of their stories.
Similarly, writers with access to 1 and 5 GenAl ideas produce stories that are evaluated more highly on usefulness by 5.5% and 11.5%, respectively.
makes a story more similar to the average of other stories within the same condition (Human with 1 GenAl idea b=0.871, p<0.001; Human with 5 GenAl ideas b=0.718, p=0.003).

Using the ownership index, evaluators ascribed 25.4% less ownership to authors who had access to one GenAl idea, relative to writers in the Human only condition (b = −1.96, p<0.001, see Table S13; compared to the Human only mean of 7.74).
The ownership discount is even higher for writers who had access to up to five GenAl ideas, at 31.0% (b = −2.40, p<0.001).
we recruited a group of N=293 participants (“writers”)
a separate group of N=600 participants (“evaluators”)
we included participants who were Prolific participants who indicated that they are based in the United Kingdom
In short, the Human with 5 Al Ideas condition effectively equalizes the creativity scores across less and more creative writers.

result.one_line: In two lab experiments with 310 online workers, access to M365 Copilot reduced time on information retrieval and content creation tasks by 27-29% without a significant change in accuracy or quality.
result.qual_notes: The study conducted two randomized controlled trials with subjects recruited from Upwork performing simulated information worker tasks. Experiment 1 focused on information retrieval, finding a 26.6% reduction in completion time. Experiment 2 involved a more diverse set of tasks (meeting recap, info retrieval, content creation) and found a 29.4% overall time reduction, with the largest gains in the creative writing task (62.6% faster). The authors note that the pre-release version of the tool used may be a conservative estimate of future productivity gains.

paper_metadata.authors: Christopher Flathmann, Beau G. Schelble, Patrick J. Rosopa, Nathan J. McNeese, Rohit Mallick, Kapil Chalil Madathil
paper_metadata.title: Examining the impact of varying levels of Al teammate influence on human-Al teams
paper_metadata.sample: 33 university students from a large southeastern US university

productivity.finding: An AI teammate that dynamically decreases its influence over time significantly improves human performance, while an AI that increases its influence stagnates or harms it.

inequality.finding: The ideal level of AI influence depends on the human's goals: players who want to learn by watching prefer a high-influence AI, while those who want to learn by doing and have more hands-on experience prefer a low-influence AI.

substitute_complement.finding: A high-influence AI teammate substitutes for human action, which can demotivate players and stagnate their skill improvement, whereas a low-influence AI complements human action, and an AI that dynamically reduces its influence over time is most effective at improving human performance.

findings.comparison: AI with dynamically decreasing influence (low end-state) vs. Game 1 score
findings.metric_definition: The difference between a player's game score in Game 3 and Game 1.
findings.note: This is the mean improvement for one condition from a significant two-way interaction between AI influence and AI variability (p < .001).

findings.comparison: AI with statically low influence vs. Game 1 score
findings.metric_definition: The difference between a player's game score in Game 3 and Game 1.
findings.note: This is the mean improvement for one condition from a significant two-way interaction between AI influence and AI variability (p < .001).

findings.comparison: AI with dynamically increasing influence (high end-state) vs. Game 1 score
findings.metric_definition: The difference between a player's game score in Game 3 and Game 1.
findings.note: This is the mean improvement for one condition from a significant two-way interaction between AI influence and AI variability (p < .001).

result.one_line: In a team-based game, an AI teammate that dynamically reduces its influence over time is most effective at improving human performance, while a persistently high-influence AI can stagnate skill development.
result.qual_notes: This study was a lab experiment with 33 university students playing the game Rocket League with a custom AI teammate. The AI's 'influence' (its tendency to take control of the ball) was manipulated to be high or low, and to be static or dynamic over three games. The key finding is that an AI that reduces its influence over time leads to the greatest improvement in human performance, suggesting a 'scaffolding' approach is optimal. The ideal level of AI influence also depends on the user's personal goals, such as whether they prefer to learn by watching or by doing.

evidence.snippet: When Al influence was low and AI variability was dynamic, this resulted in the greatest improvement (M = 55.65)
and this differed significantly from low Al influence with static AI variability (M = -5.91)
and high AI influence with dynamic AI variability (M = -33.92).
There was a statistically significant two-way interaction between AI influence and AI variability on improvement, x²(1) = 20.11 (p < .001).
Christopher Flathmann *, Beau G. Schelble, Patrick J. Rosopa, Nathan J. McNeese, Rohit Mallick, Kapil Chalil Madathil

Examining the impact of varying levels of Al teammate influence on human-Al teams

For this experiment, the popular gaming platform Rocket League (Shown in Fig. 1) was chosen.

This study utilized two different experimental manipulations: (1) the level of influence an Al teammate has in the third game; and (2) whether or not that influence was static or dynamic prior to the third game. Each of these conditions has two condition levels, resulting in a basic 2 × 2 experimental design.

paper_metadata.title: Experimental evidence on the productivity effects of generative artificial intelligence
paper_metadata.sample: 453 college-educated professionals in occupations such as marketing, grant writing, consulting, and management, recruited from Prolific.

productivity.finding: Access to ChatGPT decreased the time taken on professional writing tasks by 40% and increased average output quality by 18%.

inequality.finding: ChatGPT reduced performance inequality, more than halving the correlation between a worker's initial and subsequent performance and disproportionately boosting the output quality of lower-skilled workers.

substitute_complement.finding: ChatGPT primarily substituted for human effort, with 33% of users submitting unedited output and others performing only minimal editing, though the authors are ambivalent whether this implies labor displacement or augmentation overall.

result.one_line: In an experiment with 453 professionals on writing tasks, ChatGPT increased productivity by decreasing time spent by 40% and increasing output quality by 18%, while also reducing performance inequality between workers.
result.qual_notes: This study was a pre-registered online RCT with 453 college-educated professionals in roles like marketing and consulting, who were given occupation-specific writing tasks. The study finds strong evidence that ChatGPT substitutes for human effort, with 33% of users submitting unedited output and others performing only minimal editing. The authors note that the tasks were well-suited to ChatGPT (generic, persuasive writing) and that real-world effects might be smaller where context-specific knowledge or fact-checking is required.

paper_metadata.title: The Impact of AI on Developer Productivity: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
paper_metadata.sample: 35 professional programmers from Upwork who completed the task

productivity.finding: Developers using GitHub Copilot completed a standardized coding task 55.8% faster on average than a control group.

inequality.finding: Less experienced developers benefited more from GitHub Copilot, suggesting the tool helps compress the performance gap between novice and more experienced programmers.

entry_level.finding: The productivity gains for novice programmers suggest that AI tools like Copilot could support job transitions and expand access to software development careers.

findings.metric_definition: Time from start to the first successful commit passing all 12 tests for a task to implement an HTTP server in JavaScript.

paper_metadata.sample: 732 participants recruited from Prolific

productivity.finding: AI assistance improves participants' accuracy in an age-classification task by 6.9 percentage points, an increase of over 10% relative to the control mean.

inequality.finding: AI assistance reduces performance inequality by helping low-ability workers more, but this gap-closing effect is limited as better-calibrated workers (those with more accurate self-assessment) benefit more from AI regardless of ability.

substitute_complement.finding: The performance gains from AI depend on workers' ability to complement the tool by accurately appraising their own skills, suggesting that human calibration is a key component of human-AI complementarity.

result.one_line: In an experiment, AI assistance improves performance more for low-ability workers, but this inequality-reducing effect is limited because gains are largest for workers who are well-calibrated (accurately assess their own skills).
result.qual_notes: This lab experiment (N=732) had participants guess if a person in a photo was over 21, with a treatment group receiving an AI-powered age estimate. The study finds that while AI helps low-ability workers more, the greatest gains accrue to those who are well-calibrated (i.e., they accurately assess their own skills). The authors show in a counterfactual that if all workers were perfectly calibrated, AI would reduce performance inequality by nearly twice as much. The AI used was a predictive, not generative, model.

productivity.finding: Using an LLM as a 'sounding board' for feedback improves ad quality for non-experts, while using it as a 'ghostwriter' harms the performance of experts and provides no benefit to non-experts.

result.one_line: In a creative ad-writing task, using generative AI as a 'sounding board' for feedback boosts the performance of non-experts and closes the skill gap, while using it as a 'ghostwriter' harms the performance of experts.
result.qual_notes: This study was a 3x2 randomized experiment with 355 online participants (experts vs. non-experts) tasked with writing ad copy. The two AI conditions were a 'ghostwriter' (generates content) and a 'sounding board' (provides feedback). The key finding is that the modality of interaction is critical: feedback helps novices, but content generation hurts experts, likely due to an anchoring effect that reduces the creativity and quality of their output. The study measures ad quality via real-world ad clicks.

paper_metadata.title: AI-enhanced collective intelligence
paper_metadata.sample: Conceptual review paper.

productivity.finding: Hybrid teams of humans and AI can achieve higher performance on metrics like situational awareness and score compared to all-human teams.

substitute_complement.finding: AI is framed as a tool to enhance and complement human collective intelligence by augmenting skills and taking on various team roles, rather than replacing humans.

result.one_line: This conceptual review argues that AI enhances human collective intelligence by complementing human skills and acting in various team roles, rather than by replacing human collaborators.
result.qual_notes: This paper is a conceptual review that proposes a multilayer network framework for understanding AI-enhanced collective intelligence. It synthesizes existing literature to argue that AI primarily acts as a complement to human teams, taking on roles like assistant, teammate, coach, or manager to improve group performance. The paper does not present new empirical data but reviews various real-world applications and discusses challenges like bias, trust, and communication in human-AI teams.

evidence.snippet: Hao Cui1,2,3 and Taha Yasseri1,2,3,*
Patterns 5, November 8, 2024
AI-enhanced collective intelligence
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.patter.2024.101074
Patterns 5, November 8, 2024
Review
In this review of AI-enhanced CI, we adopted a narrative review methodology guided by a complexity-theory and network-science framework.

This review incorporates perspectives from complex network science to conceptualize a multilayer representation of human-AI collective intelligence, comprising cognition, physical, and information layers.
Finally, recent advancements in developing large language models (LLMs) have also been proposed to reshape CI.
Research on mixed teams composed of humans and AI shows hybrid teams could achieve higher performance metrics, such as team situational awareness and score, than all-human teams.
Instead, we believe that AI can enhance human collective intelligence rather than replace it.
This exploration is essential for boosting productivity, fostering innovation, and ensuring that AI complements and enhances human skills rather than replacing them outright.

This review focuses on integrating AI to bolster the CI of human groups, addressing a notable theoretical gap in understanding performance enhancements, particularly in hybrid human-AI configurations.

result.one_line: In science, AI adoption boosts individual productivity (3x more papers, 5x more citations) and accelerates junior careers, but is associated with smaller teams (especially fewer juniors) and a collective narrowing of research focus.
result.qual_notes: This observational study analyzes over 41 million scientific papers to compare outcomes for scientists who do and do not use AI. The study finds that while AI users are more productive and junior AI users advance faster, AI-augmented teams are smaller, particularly with a 31% reduction in junior scientists. A major secondary finding is that AI use is correlated with a contraction in the 'knowledge extent' of science, suggesting AI focuses research on data-rich, established problems rather than exploring new frontiers. The study's definition of AI use is based on text analysis of papers, and the analysis is correlational, not causal.

evidence.snippet: On average, researchers adopting AI annually publish 3.02 times more papers (t ≥47.18, p < 0.001 and d f > 103 in t-test on any discipline)

and garner 4.84 times more citations (t ≥30.32, p < 0.001 and d f > 103 in t-test on any discipline) compared with those not adopting AI, with consistency across disciplines and robustness for core researchers with multi-year continuous publication records41 (Fig. 2b, Extended Data Fig. 4 and Supplementary Fig. S17).
Specifically, the average number of junior scientists decreased from 2.89 in non-AI teams to 1.99 (31.14%) in AI teams (t = 19.02, p < 0.001 and d f > 103 in t-test),
while the number of established scientists decreased from 4.01 to 3.58 (10.77%) in AI teams (t = 20.82, p < 0.001 and d f > 103 in t-test).
We find that the anticipated transition time to becoming established scientists is 1.37 years shorter for AI-adopted junior scientists compared to their counterparts.
The expectation of transition time is 7.33 years for junior scientists adopting AI (R2 = 0.995) and 8.70 years for those without (R2 = 0.987).

Across all studied disciplines, the probability for AI-adopted junior scientists to transition to established scientists is 45.00%, 13.64% higher than for their counterparts who do not adopt AI (t ≥1.40, p < 0.2 and d f = 90 in t-test on 4 out of 6 disciplines).
Artificial Intelligence Tools Expand Scientists’ Impact but Contract Science’s Focus

We separate the periods in which AI was predominantly conventional machine learning, deep learning, and most recently generative designs including large language models.

paper_metadata.title: The Heterogeneous Productivity Effects of Generative AI
paper_metadata.sample: 36,358 GitHub users in Italy, Austria, France, and Spain

productivity.finding: In the short-term, access to ChatGPT decreased the output quantity and quality of less-experienced coders but increased the productivity of experienced coders on routine tasks like closing issues.

inequality.finding: ChatGPT access harmed the productivity of less-experienced developers while helping experienced developers on some tasks, thereby widening the performance gap between them.

entry_level.finding: Less-experienced developers, a proxy for entry-level workers, produced more and higher-quality code when ChatGPT was banned, suggesting the tool was a net negative for their productivity.

substitute_complement.finding: For experienced developers, ChatGPT acted as a complement for routine tasks like debugging, but for less-experienced developers, it appeared to be a poor substitute for skill, leading to lower output and quality.

result.one_line: A ban on ChatGPT in Italy increased the short-term coding output and quality of less-experienced developers, while decreasing it for experienced developers on routine tasks, suggesting the tool may harm novices and help experts in complex domains.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a difference-in-differences approach to analyze the productivity of over 36,000 GitHub users following Italy's temporary ban on ChatGPT. The findings suggest that for complex tasks like software development, generative AI can be detrimental to less-experienced workers, possibly by providing faulty output that is hard for them to detect and correct. In contrast, experienced workers saw a productivity drop on routine tasks like closing issues, suggesting AI was a useful complement for them. The effects are short-term, as the ban was brief and users quickly adopted circumvention tools like VPNs.

result.qual_notes: This study was a randomized controlled trial with 379 US-based crowd workers on Prolific performing either argumentative or creative writing tasks. paper_metadata.title: Unraveling Human-AI Teaming: A Review and Outlook
paper_metadata.sample: Not applicable (review paper)

productivity.finding: Generative AI, when integrated into human teams as an active collaborator, has the potential to enhance productivity, decision-making, and innovation by combining its computational power with human intuition and expertise.

inequality.finding: Advanced AI may reduce expertise gaps between workers, which could potentially disadvantage highly skilled individuals whose unique contributions become less distinct.

substitute_complement.finding: Agentic AI is evolving from a passive tool that substitutes for routine tasks into an active, autonomous collaborator that complements human team members, requiring new models of coordination, role specification, and task delegation.

result.one_line: This review paper proposes a new framework for human-AI teaming, arguing that agentic AI is evolving from a passive tool into an active collaborator, which requires rethinking team dynamics around coordination, role definition, and social learning.
result.qual_notes: This is a conceptual review and outlook paper that proposes a research agenda for human-AI teaming. Drawing on Team Situation Awareness (SA) theory, the authors develop a framework for understanding AI as an agentic, autonomous team member rather than a passive tool. The paper discusses the challenges and opportunities in four key areas: team formulation, coordination, maintenance, and training, highlighting the need to manage new dynamics of trust, role fluidity, and responsibility.

result.one_line: In a randomized trial with 96 Google software engineers, AI coding tools increased development speed on a complex task by about 21%, with tentative evidence that more experienced developers benefited more.
result.qual_notes: This study was a randomized controlled trial with 96 full-time software engineers at Google performing a standardized, enterprise-grade coding task. The treatment group used three internal AI-enhanced features (code completion, smart paste, natural language to code). While the simple comparison showed a significant speedup, the main estimate of a 21% speed increase was not statistically significant (p=0.086) after controlling for developer-level factors like seniority and daily coding hours. The paper also found non-significant but directionally interesting interaction effects, suggesting that more senior developers and those who code more hours per day may gain more speed from AI, potentially widening performance gaps.

evidence.snippet: we estimate a roughly 21% increase in development speed attributable to AI, controlling for other important predictors (i.e. the effect attributable to AI in our best-fit model, Model 2).

Results of this analysis indicated that developers who were in the Al exposure condition (Mean LogToT = 4.6) were significantly faster at completing the task (t(83.6) = 2.11, p = .038), compared to those who were in the no-AI exposure condition (Mean LogToT = 4.3).
These data tentatively suggest that those who spend five or more hours on development tasks per day might benefit more from AI than those who spend less time programming, daily.

paper_metadata.title: Who Benefits from AI? Self-Selection, Skill Gap, and the Hidden Costs of AI Feedback
paper_metadata.sample: 52,251 human players on the lichess.org online chess platform

productivity.finding: After accounting for self-selection, AI feedback has no average causal effect on player performance, as apparent learning gains are entirely driven by the endogenous motivation of users who choose to seek feedback.

inequality.finding: Access to AI feedback widens the skill gap because higher-skilled, motivated individuals are more likely to use it and benefit more, amplifying their existing advantages.

entry_level.finding: Lower-skilled players benefit less from AI feedback than higher-skilled players, suggesting AI may amplify rather than reduce skill-based inequalities.

substitute_complement.finding: AI feedback acts as a complement to human skill, but disproportionately benefits higher-skilled individuals and leads to a collective decrease in intellectual diversity as users converge on AI-suggested strategies.

findings.comparison: Causal effect of using AI feedback on subsequent game performance (endogeneity corrected)
findings.metric_definition: Coefficient in a regression predicting game performance, controlling for endogeneity of AI use via a control function approach.
findings.note: The paper's text states the causal effect is zero. Table 3 reports statistically significant coefficients that round to 0.00, but the authors' interpretation is a null effect.
These are descriptive (not causal) effects that capture the realized relationship in the population, which drives the widening skill gap.

findings.metric: Likelihood of seeking AI feedback after a loss
findings.comparison: Interaction between player skill and having lost a game
findings.metric_definition: Interaction term coefficient in a logistic regression predicting the log-odds of seeking AI feedback.
findings.note: This finding shows that higher-skilled players are disproportionately more likely to seek feedback after failures, a key mechanism for skill gap widening.

findings.metric: Intellectual diversity (1 - Gini of chess openings)
findings.comparison: Effect of an AI-related platform update vs. before the update
findings.metric_definition: Coefficient from a regression discontinuity design comparing platform-level diversity of chess openings before and after platform updates related to the AI feedback feature.

result.one_line: In a study of chess players, AI feedback does not causally improve performance, as apparent gains are due to self-selection by motivated, higher-skilled players, which in turn widens the overall skill gap.
result.qual_notes: This study uses observational data from over 52,000 players on a public chess platform to study who chooses to use an AI feedback tool. The authors use a control function approach to account for the endogeneity of AI use, finding that motivation, not the AI tool itself, drives performance gains. A key secondary finding is that AI feedback causally reduces the intellectual diversity of strategies at the population level, as players converge on AI-recommended lines of play.

evidence.snippet: Once we control for these endogenous channels, the direct causal effect of all forms of AI feedback are zero (total AI feedback, Al feedback on losses, AI feedback on wins).

The effect of AI feedback is significantly higher for high-skilled individuals than for low-skilled individuals (based on median split: low = 0.021 [95% CI:-0.004] vs. high = 0.037 [95% CI:-0.005999999999999998]).

The effect of AI feedback is significantly higher for high-skilled individuals than for low-skilled individuals (based on median split: low = 0.021 [95% CI:-0.004] vs. high = 0.037 [95% CI:-0.005999999999999998]).

Furthermore, we find substantial heterogeneity across skill: higher-skilled individuals are much more likely to seek AI feedback in situations of failure (large and significant coefficient of the interaction term β = 0.24; p < 0.001 in Model 2).
We find AI related platform updates cause a significant decrease in intellectual diversity (Table 4: e.g., β =− 0.14; p = 0.043 in Model 1).
The final dataset contains 403,010 game-level observations from 52,251 human players.

Using data from over five years and 52,000 individuals on an online chess platform, we show that motivated and higher-skilled individuals self-select into AI feedback use—and use it more productively.

result.one_line: In two experiments, exposure to an AI assistant produced 'cognitive spillover,' causally influencing human teammates' subsequent language, attention, and social cohesion, even when the AI was no longer present.
result.qual_notes: The paper reports on two lab experiments. Study 1 (N=97) had individuals interact with a ChatGPT assistant (empathic vs. formal) and then measured linguistic spillover in a debriefing interview. Study 2 (N=69 in 20 teams) had teams perform a collaborative puzzle with a voice-based AI assistant (helpful vs. unhelpful; human vs. robotic voice) and measured effects on language, attention, mental models, and social cohesion. A key finding is that these spillover effects are robust and often implicit, occurring even when users do not trust the AI or consider it a team member; for instance, an unhelpful robotic AI paradoxically strengthened human team cohesion by creating an 'us-vs-them' dynamic. The authors note that the lab setting and scripted AI in Study 2 may not capture the full complexity of real-world workplaces, and the study was designed to identify causal spillover, not estimate its base rate in naturalistic settings.

paper_metadata.title: When GenAI increases inequality: evidence from a university debating competition
paper_metadata.sample: 142 undergraduate students at two universities in Spain

productivity.finding: Generative AI has a small and statistically insignificant positive effect on the overall performance of undergraduate students in a debating competition.

inequality.finding: Generative AI increases performance inequality, with high-ability students benefiting significantly more than low-ability students in a complex debating task.

substitute_complement.finding: For complex tasks requiring higher-order skills like debating, generative AI acts as a complement to human ability, particularly for high-skilled individuals who are better able to extract and use the information it provides.

result.one_line: In a university debating competition, generative AI increased performance inequality, significantly boosting the scores of high-ability students while having no effect on low-ability students.
result.qual_notes: This study was a field RCT with 142 undergraduate students in Spain, who were randomly assigned access to ChatGPT to prepare for debates. The key finding is that AI is a complement to skill in this complex, higher-order task, contrary to findings in more routine tasks where AI often helps novices most. High-ability students were better at using AI to improve argument credibility, rhetoric, and refutation, while low-ability students only saw a small benefit in the clarity of their presentation. The overall average productivity effect was small and not statistically significant.

evidence.snippet: Treatment debaters are 9.2% more likely to win than control debaters and score on average 2.2% (equivalent to an increase by 0.15 standard deviations) higher than control individuals, but the differences are not statistically different from zero.

I run a randomized controlled trial (RCT) to evaluate the effects of ChatGPT (the most widely used GenAI system) on debating performance in a university debating com- petition, involving 142 undergraduate students.

I run a randomized controlled trial (RCT) to evaluate the effects of ChatGPT (the most widely used GenAI system) on debating performance in a university debating com- petition, involving 142 undergraduate students.
The intervention took place at ESADE Business School and at CUNEF University over three different debating days.
This study was preregistered at the AEA registry for randomized controlled trials, with register number AEARCTR-0011113. See https://www.socialscienceregistry.org/trials/11113.
These findings suggest that for tasks such as debating that require higher-order skills, GenAI is complementary to ability and may increase inequalities in productivity.
Contrary to most early findings in the GenAI literature, I find that high ability students benefit significantly more from GenAI than their lower ability counterparts.
I find that ChatGPT has a positive but not significant effect on overall debating performance.

paper_metadata.sample: 7,637 open-source software projects on GitHub

productivity.finding: GitHub Copilot increases project-level code contributions on open-source projects by 5.9% but also increases the time required for code coordination and review by 8.0%.

inequality.finding: Core developers benefit more from Copilot than peripheral developers, increasing their share of code contributions by 6.5% and facing a relatively smaller increase in coordination time.

entry_level.finding: Peripheral developers, who have less project familiarity and are analogous to more junior roles, experience smaller productivity gains and larger increases in coordination time compared to core developers.

substitute_complement.finding: AI complements developers' coding tasks but increases coordination burdens, leading to an 8% increase in the time to merge code, driven by a 6.5% increase in code discussion volume.

result.one_line: In open-source software development, GitHub Copilot increases code contributions by 5.9% but also increases coordination time by 8.0%, with core developers benefiting more than peripheral developers.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a generalized synthetic control method on observational data from 7,637 GitHub projects between 2021 and 2022. The productivity gain is driven by both increased developer participation (+3.4%) and higher individual output (+2.1%). The increased coordination time is attributed to more code discussions. The paper finds that core developers, who have greater project familiarity, see larger productivity gains and smaller coordination burdens than peripheral developers, widening the performance gap between them.

result.one_line: In a study with graduate students, generative AI assistance combined with instruction reduced writing time by 64.5% and improved assignment grades from a B+ to an A, with a leveling effect that helped ESL students close performance gaps with native English speakers.
result.qual_notes: This study used a quasi-experimental design with 27 graduate students who completed a professional writing task without AI, then received instruction, and then completed a similar task with AI. The observed leveling effect for ESL students in both speed and quality was not statistically significant, likely due to the small sample size. The instruction on how to use genAI was a key part of the intervention, making it difficult to isolate the effect of the tool alone from the effect of the training.

paper_metadata.title: When combinations of humans and AI are useful: A systematic review and meta-analysis
paper_metadata.sample: Meta-analysis of 370 effect sizes from 106 experimental studies with participants including doctors, crowdworkers, and students.

productivity.finding: On average, human-AI combinations perform significantly better than humans alone (human augmentation) but worse than the best of either humans or AI alone (no synergy).

inequality.finding: The analysis found that whether participants were experts or not did not significantly affect the performance of human-AI systems.

substitute_complement.finding: Human-AI teams on average complement human workers by outperforming them, but they do not achieve synergy, performing worse than the best of either the human or the AI alone.

findings.note: This is the effect on 'augmentation' when the AI is initially better than the human.

result.one_line: A meta-analysis of 106 experiments finds that while human-AI teams outperform humans alone, they underperform the best of either the human or AI, with synergy only appearing when humans are initially better than the AI and on creative tasks.
result.qual_notes: This paper is a meta-analysis of 370 effect sizes from 106 experiments on human-AI collaboration published between 2020 and 2023. The primary finding is that 'human augmentation' (Human-AI > Human) is common, but 'human-AI synergy' (Human-AI > max(Human, AI)) is not, and on average is negative. Synergy is more likely in creative tasks than decision tasks, and when humans initially outperform the AI. The study's scope is limited to experiments that measure performance for all three groups (human, AI, human-AI), potentially excluding tasks where humans or AI cannot perform the task alone.

entry_level.finding: Early-career authors who received AI feedback were significantly more likely to revise their manuscripts, with an 18.9% relative increase in revisions compared to no significant effect for later-career authors.

substitute_complement.finding: The study frames the LLM as an 'AI collaborator' that provides scientific feedback, a task typically performed by human peers, suggesting it can act as a substitute for or expand access to this form of collaboration.

result.one_line: In a large field experiment with over 31,000 scientific preprints, providing LLM-generated feedback increased manuscript revisions by 12.55%, with the largest benefits accruing to early-career, lower-prominence, and non-English-speaking researchers.
result.qual_notes: This global randomized field experiment sent customized LLM-generated feedback to authors of arXiv preprints. The primary outcome was the rate of manuscript revision within one month, a proxy for engagement and quality improvement. The study finds that AI feedback not only increases revisions but also disproportionately benefits researchers with less access to traditional feedback networks, suggesting an equalizing effect. A secondary finding is that exposure to AI feedback also increased the subsequent adoption of LLM tools in authors' future work.

paper_metadata.title: AI Is Already Eroding Wages: Quasi-Experimental Evidence From Occupational Exposure
paper_metadata.sample: 138 million U.S. workers from the Revelio Technologies dataset

productivity.finding: The automation potential of generative AI appears to dominate any productivity gains, leading to an average 4.5% fall in starting wages for new positions in exposed firms.

inequality.finding: Generative AI widens wage inequality within firms, as starting wages for junior and mid-level workers fall by 6.3% and 5.9% respectively, while senior workers' wages remain stable or increase.

entry_level.finding: AI-exposed firms reduce demand for entry-level labor, decreasing the share of new junior positions by 4 percentage points and cutting junior starting wages by 6.3%.

substitute_complement.finding: AI acts as a substitute for junior-level labor, causing firms to reduce hiring for junior roles while increasing the share of new mid-level positions by a similar amount.

result.one_line: In firms with high exposure to generative AI, the arrival of ChatGPT led to a 4.5% average decline in starting wages, driven by significant wage drops for junior (-6.3%) and mid-level (-5.9%) workers, while senior wages remained stable.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a difference-in-differences design on a large dataset of 138 million U.S. worker profiles to estimate the causal impact of ChatGPT's release on firm wage and hiring structures. The key finding is a seniority-biased wage decline, where junior and mid-level workers' starting wages fall while seniors' are unaffected, widening inequality. Exposed firms also shift hiring away from junior roles towards mid-level roles, suggesting AI substitutes for routine cognitive tasks concentrated at the bottom of the career ladder.

paper_metadata.title: Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Experienced Open-Source Developer Productivity
paper_metadata.sample: 16 developers with moderate AI experience completing 246 tasks in mature open-source projects

productivity.finding: In a field RCT with experienced open-source developers, access to generative AI tools increased task completion time by 19%, a significant productivity decrease.

inequality.finding: The productivity-reducing effect of AI was larger for experienced developers working on tasks where they had high prior exposure, suggesting AI is less helpful for experts on familiar work.

substitute_complement.finding: AI tools reallocated developers' time, substituting for active coding while introducing new tasks like prompting, waiting for, and reviewing AI outputs.

findings.metric_definition: Percentage change in total implementation time for a task, estimated via a log-linear regression.
findings.note: This is a regression-adjusted estimate controlling for task difficulty. The raw difference was a 34% increase in time.

findings.dimension: Time spent reviewing and cleaning AI generated outputs
findings.note: Measured on a subset of 44 issues with valid screen recordings.

findings.dimension: Time spent waiting on AI generated outputs
findings.note: Measured on a subset of 44 issues with valid screen recordings.
Developers' forecast before starting tasks.

findings.metric: Post-hoc estimated change in completion time
findings.note: Developers' estimate after completing the study.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with experienced open-source developers, access to generative AI tools surprisingly increased task completion time by 19%, contradicting both developer and expert expectations of a speedup.
result.qual_notes: This RCT involved 16 experienced developers working on 246 real tasks in large, mature open-source repositories they were familiar with. The observed 19% slowdown was robust, and the paper suggests it may be driven by factors like high developer familiarity with the codebase and the complexity of the repositories, which limit AI's effectiveness. The study also finds that AI reallocates developer time from coding to prompting and reviewing AI output.

evidence.snippet: Surprisingly, we find that allowing AI actually increases completion time by 19%—AI tooling slowed developers down.

Quantitatively, on the subset of 44 issues with valid labeled screen recordings, we find that when AI is allowed, developers spend approximately 9% of their time reviewing and cleaning AI generated outputs when working with AI.

Quantitatively, on the subset of 44 issues with valid labeled screen recordings, we find that when AI is allowed, developers spend approximately 4% of their time waiting on AI generated outputs when working with AI.
Before starting tasks, developers forecast that allowing AI will reduce completion time by 24%.
After completing the study, developers estimate that allowing AI reduced completion time by 20%.

paper_metadata.title: Canaries in the Coal Mine? Six Facts about the Recent Employment Effects of Artificial Intelligence
paper_metadata.sample: 3.5 to 5 million workers per month from ADP, the largest payroll provider in the United States.

inequality.finding: Generative AI disproportionately harms early-career workers (ages 22-25), whose employment in exposed occupations declined, while employment for experienced workers in the same occupations remained stable or grew.

entry_level.finding: In occupations highly exposed to generative AI, employment for early-career workers (ages 22-25) experienced a 16% relative decline after controlling for firm-level shocks.

substitute_complement.finding: Employment declines for young workers are concentrated in occupations where AI primarily automates tasks (substitution), whereas occupations where AI augments human work (complementation) saw employment growth.

findings.comparison: Relative to less-exposed occupations, controlling for firm-level shocks
findings.metric_definition: The percentage change in employment for early-career workers in AI-exposed occupations relative to those in non-exposed occupations, after accounting for firm-time fixed effects.
findings.note: This is the main finding highlighted in the abstract and conclusion.

findings.comparison: Most AI-exposed quintiles vs least exposed quintile, controlling for firm-time effects
findings.metric_definition: The coefficient from a Poisson event study regression, representing the log-point change in employment for the most exposed quintiles relative to the least exposed.
findings.note: The paper reports this as a '15 log point decline'. The effect is described as 'large and statistically significant'.

findings.comparison: Employment level in Sep 2025 vs late 2022
findings.group: Workers aged 22-25 in the most AI-exposed occupations
findings.metric_definition: The raw percentage change in employment for this group between late 2022 and September 2025.
findings.note: This is a descriptive statistic, not a regression-adjusted estimate.

findings.comparison: Employment level in Sep 2025 vs late 2022
findings.group: Older workers in the most AI-exposed occupations
findings.metric_definition: The raw percentage change in employment for this group between late 2022 and September 2025.
findings.note: This contrasts with the 6% decline for younger workers in the same occupations over the same period.

result.one_line: Using high-frequency payroll data, this study finds that since the advent of generative AI, early-career workers (ages 22-25) in AI-exposed occupations have experienced significant employment declines, while experienced workers' employment has remained stable.
result.qual_notes: This observational study uses a large administrative payroll dataset from ADP to analyze US labor market trends from 2021-2025. The core finding is a 16% relative employment decline for young workers (22-25) in AI-exposed occupations, controlling for firm-level shocks. The paper distinguishes between AI that automates (substitutes for labor), which is associated with employment declines, and AI that augments (complements labor), which is not. The authors find that these adjustments occur through employment quantity rather than wages, which show little change.

evidence.snippet: Early-career workers (ages 22-25) in AI-exposed occupations experienced 16% relative employment declines, controlling for firm-level shocks, while employment for experienced workers remained stable.
After conditioning on firm-time effects, young workers experienced a 16% relative employment decline in the most exposed occupations.

In contrast, workers aged 22 to 25 have experienced a 6% decline in employment from late 2022 to September 2025 in the most AI-exposed occupations, compared to a 6-9% increase for older workers.

paper_metadata.sample: 5,172 customer-support agents at a Fortune 500 firm

productivity.finding: Generative AI assistance increased customer support agent productivity (issues resolved per hour) by 15.2%, driven by faster handling times and an increased capacity for multitasking.

entry_level.finding: AI assistance significantly accelerates on-the-job learning for new workers, with treated agents at two months of tenure performing as well as untreated agents with over six months of tenure.

substitute_complement.finding: The AI tool was designed to augment rather than replace human agents, acting as a complement by providing real-time suggestions that agents could choose to adopt, edit, or ignore.

result.one_line: A generative AI assistant for customer support agents increased productivity by 15% on average, with the largest gains for low-skill and novice workers, compressing the performance gap and accelerating on-the-job learning.
result.qual_notes: This study of 5,172 customer support agents at a Fortune 500 firm uses a staggered rollout of a GPT-3 based conversational assistant to estimate its causal effects. The AI tool acts as a complement to human labor, disproportionately benefiting less-skilled and less-experienced workers and enabling them to learn faster. The study also finds that AI assistance improves customer sentiment and reduces agent attrition, particularly for new hires, suggesting positive effects on the experience of work.

paper_metadata.title: The Effects of Generative AI on High-Skilled Work: Evidence from Three Field Experiments with Software Developers
paper_metadata.sample: 4,867 software developers at Microsoft, Accenture, and an anonymous Fortune 100 company.

productivity.finding: Using a generative AI coding assistant increased the number of completed tasks (pull requests) by 26% for software developers across three large companies.

inequality.finding: Generative AI disproportionately benefited less experienced developers, with junior and short-tenure developers increasing their output by 21-40% compared to 7-16% for senior, long-tenure developers.

entry_level.finding: Generative AI provides larger productivity gains to junior and recently hired software developers, suggesting it enhances rather than removes the value of entry-level roles.

substitute_complement.finding: The AI coding assistant acts as a complement to human labor, augmenting the productivity of individual software developers rather than substituting for them.

findings.metric_definition: Weekly number of completed tasks (pull requests), a unit of work for software developers.
findings.note: This is a precision-weighted average LATE (local average treatment effect) from three field experiments, instrumenting AI adoption with random assignment.

findings.metric_definition: Weekly number of code updates (commits).
findings.note: This is a precision-weighted average LATE from three field experiments. Effect is not statistically significant.

findings.metric_definition: Weekly number of times code was compiled.
findings.note: This is a precision-weighted average LATE from three field experiments.

Short-tenure developers increased their output by 27% to 39%, while long-tenure developers experienced smaller gains of 8% to 13%.
Junior developers increased their output by 21% to 40%, while senior developers had more marginal gains of 7% to 16%.

result.one_line: In three field experiments with 4,867 software developers, a generative AI coding assistant increased task completion by 26% and disproportionately benefited less experienced and junior employees.
result.qual_notes: This study analyzes data from three randomized controlled trials with software developers at Microsoft (US), Accenture (Southeast Asia), and an anonymous company. The main productivity estimate is a local average treatment effect (LATE) from a weighted instrumental variable regression, finding that access to GitHub Copilot increased completed tasks (pull requests) by 26%. The paper finds that less experienced developers (by tenure, job level, and pre-experiment productivity) saw larger gains, suggesting AI can compress skill gaps. There was no evidence of decreased code quality at Microsoft; in fact, pull request approval rates increased by 9.9%.

productivity.finding: Generative AI significantly improves the quality of solutions for new product development tasks by 0.37-0.39 standard deviations and reduces task completion time by 13-16%.

inequality.finding: Generative AI disproportionately benefits workers less familiar with a task, allowing them to achieve performance levels comparable to experienced teams and thus compressing the performance gap.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI acts as a 'cybernetic teammate' that substitutes for a human collaborator, as individuals using AI achieve solution quality comparable to two-person teams working without AI.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with 776 professionals, generative AI acts as a 'cybernetic teammate,' enabling individuals to match the performance of two-person teams and helping less-experienced workers achieve expert-level outcomes.
result.qual_notes: This field experiment at Procter & Gamble randomly assigned 776 professionals to work on new product development tasks individually or in teams, with or without access to GPT-4. The study finds that AI can substitute for a human teammate, as AI-assisted individuals performed on par with two-person teams. AI also acted as an equalizer, allowing employees less familiar with the task to achieve quality levels comparable to experienced teams. A key caveat is that participants were relatively inexperienced with AI, so the observed effects may be a lower bound on potential productivity gains.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with 7,137 knowledge workers, access to a generative AI assistant reduced time spent on email by 12% (1.4 hours/week) and time working outside of regular hours, without changing the quantity or composition of other work tasks.
result.qual_notes: This large-scale, cross-industry field RCT studied the effects of providing Microsoft 365 Copilot to knowledge workers over six months. The primary impact was time savings on email, with some of that time reallocated to workers as leisure (reduced after-hours work). The study found no significant changes in time spent in meetings or writing documents, nor in the quantity of outputs, suggesting AI acted as a complement to speed up existing tasks rather than a substitute that reallocated labor. The authors also found no evidence that effects differed by workers' pre-period behaviors.

evidence.snippet: Our intent-to-treat (LATE) estimates show workers with access to Copilot spent an average of 1.4 (2) fewer hours per week in email sessions over post-period months 4-6, a 12% (17%) decline from their pre-period average.
In the pre-period, the average worker spent 11.7 hours per week reading and responding to emails.

Across applications, the last row of the table shows workers spent a quarter of an hour less per week working outside of their modal work hours, a 9% reduction, with somewhat larger time savings in after-hours email work.

result.one_line: Using near real-time US labor market data, this study finds that higher occupational exposure to generative AI is associated with reduced employment, higher unemployment, and shorter work hours, with effects concentrated in analytical cognitive roles and among younger and older workers.
result.qual_notes: This study constructs a dynamic occupational AI exposure score using LLMs (ChatGPT-4o, Claude 3.5) and links it to US Current Population Survey data from Oct 2022 to March 2025. The analysis is a first-differenced regression at the occupation level, identifying correlations rather than causal effects. Key heterogeneities are found: workers in manual-physical roles see employment gains, while those in analytical-cognitive roles see losses. College-educated workers experience smaller employment declines but larger shifts in work intensity and job structure.

evidence.snippet: In the fully specified model, a 10 point increase in exposure is associated with a 5.6 percentage point decline in total occupational employment (s.e. 2.0 ppt)
and a 0.64 percentage point increase in unemployment (s.e. 0.26 ppt) when using ChatGPT-generated scores
Using ChatGPT-based exposure scores, a 10 point increase in exposure is associated with a reduction of approximately 0.31 hours worked at the main job (s.e. 0.15 hours)
and a 0.93 percentage point decline in the share of full-time workers (s.e. 0.58).

A 10 point increase in AI exposure is associated with employment gains of 10.1 percentage points for top-quartile occupations using ChatGPT-based exposure scores, compared to a 6.8 point decline (s.e. 2.0 ppt) for non-top-quartile occupations, implying a relatively precisely estimated differential effect of +16.9 percentage points (s.e. 5.5 ppt).

Using Claude-generated exposure scores, a 10-point increase in exposure is associated with a 6.19 percentage point decline in employment for workers aged 30–50, compared to a 7.67 percentage point decline for workers under 30
and a 6.66 percentage point decline for those over 50.
using ChatGPT-generated scores, a 10-point increase in exposure is associated with a 9.3 percentage point decline in total employment for workers without a bachelor's degree,
compared to a 7.5 percentage point decline for those with a bachelor's degree or more.

substitute_complement.finding: Survey results indicate workers used AI as a complement to their own skills, with 50% combining AI output with their own knowledge and only 33% using the AI response directly as their final answer.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with public sector knowledge workers, generative AI increased the quality (+17%) and speed (+34%) of a document comprehension task, especially for lower-performers, but decreased quality (-12%) on a data analysis task.
result.qual_notes: This study randomized 143 staff at the Central Bank of Ireland to use a GPT-4-based tool for either a document comprehension or data analysis task. The productivity effects were highly task-dependent, with AI helping on the document task but hurting on the data task. For the document task, AI compressed performance differences, boosting the quality of lower-skilled workers while slightly decreasing the quality of higher-skilled workers. Survey data suggests workers used AI as a complement, with a majority editing or combining AI output with their own knowledge.

paper_metadata.title: Artificial Intelligence and the Labor Market
paper_metadata.sample: Approximately 58 million LinkedIn profiles and 14 million job postings linked to publicly traded Compustat firms in the U.S.

productivity.finding: AI adoption at the firm level leads to significant growth, with a one standard deviation increase in AI use associated with a 7.5 percentage point increase in total factor productivity over five years.

inequality.finding: AI exposure is highest for high-wage occupations, but due to offsetting firm productivity and task reallocation effects, these jobs see a slight net increase in their aggregate employment share compared to low-wage jobs.

substitute_complement.finding: AI substitutes for labor at the task level, but this is counteracted by complementary effects, as workers reallocate to non-exposed tasks and AI-adopting firms grow, leading to muted net employment changes.

result.one_line: AI substitutes for labor at the task level, but these losses are offset by workers reallocating to non-automated tasks and by overall firm growth, resulting in modest net employment effects that slightly favor high-wage occupations.
result.qual_notes: The study uses a novel dataset of AI applications from 58 million resumes (2014-2023) to measure firm- and occupation-specific AI exposure. It finds that while average task exposure reduces an occupation's employment (substitution), concentrated exposure that allows for task reallocation mitigates this effect (complementarity). A key caveat is that the data largely pre-dates the rise of generative AI. The causal estimates rely on an instrumental variable based on firms' historical university hiring networks.

evidence.snippet: focusing on the IV results, a one–standard deviation increase in our AI adoption measure leads to a 9.7 percentage point increase in firm revenue growth and a 7.5 percentage point increase in total factor productivity over the subsequent five years.
Importantly, we find a positive effect of AI adoption on employment: a one-standard deviation increase in AI adoption leads to a 6.8 percentage point increase in firm employment.
In our most saturated specification (column 4), a one–standard deviation increase in task-level AI exposure lowers demand for the associated skills by about 2 percent over five years.
in our preferred specification, a one–standard deviation increase in mean task exposure reduces the occupation’s within-firm employment share by about 14.5 percent over five years,
while a similar increase in concentration raises it by about 7.5 percent.
At the other end of the distribution, low-wage jobs see modest employment gains of roughly 2%.

The direct substitution effect is again strongest for high-paying jobs: at the 90th percentile, within-firm employment falls by about 2.8% over five years. Concentration of exposure across tasks tempers this decline to about 1.5%.
At the very top, the net effect is positive at around 3 percent over a 5-year period, despite substantial direct exposure for these occupations.
Our measure of AI adoption ends in 2023 and therefore largely excludes the recent rise of generative AI (GenAI).

result.one_line: In an experiment with 1,041 UK workers, ChatGPT increased productivity in text-based tasks but did not reduce performance inequality between demographic groups, with more frequent AI users benefiting more on complex and ambiguous tasks.
result.qual_notes: This study conducted a pre-registered online experiment with 1,041 UK workers who already had a ChatGPT account. Participants were randomly assigned to use or not use ChatGPT on three text-based tasks of varying complexity and ambiguity. The study finds that while AI boosts performance and speed, the magnitude of the gain depends on task characteristics (complexity and ambiguity), not just complexity alone. Contrary to the 'great equalizer' narrative, the paper finds no evidence that AI compresses performance gaps between gender, age, education, or occupational groups, and finds that more frequent AI users (a proxy for skill) gain more on harder tasks.

paper_metadata.title: Still Waters, Rapid Currents: Early Labor Market Transformation under Generative AI
paper_metadata.sample: 24,796 workers in 11 highly exposed occupations (accountants, customer support, financial advisors, HR, IT support, journalists, legal, marketing, office clerks, software developers, teachers) in Denmark, from a 2024 survey linked to administrative records.

productivity.finding: Despite self-reported productivity benefits, generative AI has a precisely null effect on workers' earnings and hours, with estimates ruling out average effects larger than 2% two years after ChatGPT's launch.

inequality.finding: Generative AI facilitates occupational mobility, allowing adopters who switch jobs to see 12 percentage points faster earnings growth and move into higher-paying occupations where AI is more relevant.

entry_level.finding: Contrary to concerns about displacement, the adoption of AI chatbots by firms does not drive recent aggregate declines in early-career employment.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI primarily complements labor by reorganizing work and creating new tasks, with no net effect on total employment, job churn, or incumbent worker hours at adopting firms.

findings.metric_definition: Difference-in-differences estimate of the effect of AI chatbot adoption on worker log earnings.
findings.note: The pooled difference-in-differences estimates are precise zeros, with confidence bands ruling out effects larger than 2%.

findings.comparison: Adopting vs. non-adopting workplaces
findings.metric_definition: Difference-in-differences estimate of the effect of firm AI adoption on the employment share of early-career workers (ages 22-25).
findings.note: Estimates are precise enough to rule out effects greater than a third of a percentage point (0.33pp).

findings.comparison: AI chatbot adopters vs. non-adopters, by Dec 2024
findings.metric_definition: Increase in full-time-equivalent (FTE) employment in a worker's most recent occupation, indicating a switch from their original occupation.
findings.note: This finding suggests AI facilitates occupational mobility.

findings.comparison: Adopters who switch occupations vs. other Danish workers (Nov 2022 - Dec 2024)
findings.metric_definition: The additional earnings growth for AI adopters who switch occupations compared to the average for all Danish workers employed in both Nov 2022 and Dec 2024.

findings.comparison: Workplaces encouraging chatbot use vs. those not
findings.metric_definition: Difference-in-differences estimate of the effect of firm AI adoption on total hours worked.
findings.note: Estimates are precise, ruling out effects larger than 2%.

findings.comparison: Workplaces encouraging chatbot use vs. those not
findings.metric_definition: Difference-in-differences estimate of the effect of firm AI adoption on job churn (hires and separations).
findings.note: Confidence bands rule out effects on monthly job churn rates greater than 0.5% of employment.

result.one_line: Using linked survey-administrative data from Denmark, this paper finds that while generative AI is widely adopted and leads to significant work reorganization, it has had precisely zero effect on earnings, hours, or employment at either the worker or firm level two years after ChatGPT's launch.
result.qual_notes: The study links large-scale surveys of 24,796 workers in 11 exposed occupations to Danish administrative records. Using a difference-in-differences design, it finds no impact of AI adoption on earnings or employment, including for early-career workers, ruling out effects larger than 2-3%. The only significant labor market effect is increased occupational mobility for AI adopters, who move into higher-paying occupations where AI is more relevant. The authors argue that firms are absorbing the technology through internal task reorganization rather than by adjusting employment or wages.

evidence.snippet: our pooled difference-in-differences estimates rule out effects larger than 2%.

Difference-in-differences estimates show no differential changes in the employment share of early-career workers at adopting workplaces, with estimates precise enough to rule out effects greater than a third of a percentage point.

since the arrival of AI chatbots, adopters are significantly more likely to have switched occupations, working about 4% of a full-time equivalent more in their most recent occupation by December 2024.
Adopters who switch occupations see 12 percentage points faster earnings growth between November 2022 and December 2024 relative to other Danish workers employed in both months.

Panel (a) shows that workplaces encouraging chatbot use have seen no differential change in total hours after the arrival of AI chatbots.20 Our estimates are precise, ruling out effects larger than 2%.

Panel (d) examines overall rates of job creation and destruction, showing that job churn at workplaces that encourage AI chatbots has remained stable, with confidence bands ruling out effects on monthly job churn rates greater than 0.5% of employment.

paper_metadata.title: From helping hand to stumbling block: the ChatGPT paradox in competency experiment
paper_metadata.sample: 349 Korean-speaking university students

productivity.finding: ChatGPT significantly decreased performance on a mathematical problem-solving task, while having no statistically significant average effect on reading/writing or computational thinking tasks.

inequality.finding: Low-ability participants were harmed by ChatGPT on a math task as they could not discern correct from incorrect answers, while intermediate-ability participants benefited on a writing task, contesting the idea that AI always complements the lowest-skilled workers.

findings.comparison: ChatGPT vs No-AI control
findings.metric_definition: Average score on a mathematical problem-solving task (M-task).
findings.note: The paper reports a statistically significant decrease but does not provide the mean scores or the magnitude of the effect.

findings.metric: Average score on reading and writing task
findings.comparison: ChatGPT vs No-AI control
findings.metric_definition: Average score on a reading and writing task (W-task).
findings.note: The average scores were not statistically different between the treatment and control groups.

findings.metric: Average score on computational thinking task
findings.comparison: ChatGPT vs No-AI control
findings.metric_definition: Average score on a computational thinking task (C-task).
findings.note: The average scores were not statistically different between the treatment and control groups.

findings.metric: Performance on writing task by skill level
findings.note: On the writing task, intermediate-ability participants (between the 40th and 95th percentile) benefited from ChatGPT, while low- and high-ability participants did not.

findings.metric: Performance on math task by skill level
findings.note: On the math task, low-ability participants were harmed by ChatGPT because they were less able to distinguish the AI's incorrect 'hallucinated' answers from correct ones.

result.one_line: In a lab experiment with students, ChatGPT significantly decreased performance on a mathematical problem-solving task, particularly for low-ability participants, while having no average effect on writing or computational thinking tasks.
result.qual_notes: This lab experiment with 349 university students tested ChatGPT's effect on three tasks: reading/writing, math, and computational thinking. While average performance did not change for writing and computational thinking, it significantly decreased for the math task. The negative effect on math was driven by low-ability participants who could not discern the AI's plausible but incorrect answers. Conversely, on the writing task, intermediate-ability participants saw performance gains, while low- and high-ability participants did not, suggesting a complex, context-dependent impact on skill inequality.

evidence.snippet: For the M-task, the average score of the treatment group is also significantly lower than the control group (p-value = 0.002).
In the W-task, the null hypothesis that the average score of the treatment group is the same as the control group cannot be rejected (t-test, p-value = 0.138)

Regarding the C-task, no significant difference was found between the control and treatment groups. The null hypothesis on the draws from the same population is not rejected (p-value = 0.482), and the mean score difference is statistically insignificant (p-value = 0.544).

We found that the reading and writing task score in the treatment group is statistically indifferent from that in the control group, but the score distribution is more left-skewed with the same support, suggesting that intermediate-ability subjects benefit, but high or low-ability subjects don't.

In the interval between the bottom 40% and 95%, the distribution of the control group (dashed line) is to the left of the treatment group, suggesting that participants with competence in this interval achieved better scores with ChatGPT's assistance.
This result is mainly because subjects with low maths ability couldn't discern ChatGPT's plausible (but incorrect) responses from the correct ones.
