We ran a controlled laboratory experiment to examine whether ChatGPT's aid can increase the participants' performance in three different – reading and writing, mathematical problem-solving, and computational thinking – tasks.
This section reports the results of our experiment, which involved a total of 349 participants, 181 treated, focusing on how much the task scores improved with ChatGPT's assistance.

paper_metadata.title: Generative AI and Labor Market Outcomes: Evidence from the United Kingdom
paper_metadata.sample: 26,979 UK firms (from Revelio Labs data) and 63.5 million UK job postings from 2021-2025.

inequality.finding: Generative AI's effects are concentrated in high-wage segments, with high-compensation firms and high-salary occupations experiencing larger employment and hiring reductions, suggesting a potential compression of wage inequality.

entry_level.finding: Firms highly exposed to generative AI reduce employment primarily in junior positions, with low-seniority employment declining by 0.4% per standard deviation of exposure while senior roles are less affected.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI acts as a substitute for labor in highly exposed firms, reducing overall employment and hiring, particularly for technical and creative roles, while increasing hiring for customer-facing sales roles.

result.one_line: In the UK, firms highly exposed to generative AI reduced employment and hiring, especially for junior and high-wage technical roles, suggesting AI substitutes for these workers and may compress wage inequality.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a difference-in-differences design on UK firm employment and job posting data from 2021-2025, measuring AI exposure based on pre-ChatGPT occupational mix. The findings show that AI exposure leads to employment declines concentrated in junior and high-wage positions, reducing hiring for technical roles while increasing it for sales roles. Caveats include the use of potential exposure rather than actual adoption and a focus on a 30-month post-treatment window, which may only capture short-term effects.

paper_metadata.title: Human-AI Teams' Impact on Organizations – A Review
paper_metadata.sample: A systematic literature review of 122 papers on human-AI teams in white-collar organizations.

productivity.finding: AI adoption in teams is primarily implemented to increase work efficiency and decrease employee workloads, leading to productivity gains.

entry_level.finding: The implementation of human-AI teams is expected to cause a change in human role structure, including a potential reduction of human junior analyst roles.

substitute_complement.finding: AI complements human workers by completing repetitive and routine tasks, which alters human roles and enables people to move into more strategic positions.

result.one_line: This literature review finds that AI in teams complements human workers by automating routine tasks, which reallocates humans to more strategic roles and may reduce demand for junior positions.
result.qual_notes: This paper is a systematic literature review of 122 articles on human-AI teams (HAITs) in white-collar organizations. It identifies key success factors (e.g., playing to human/AI strengths), common use cases (e.g., decision-making, routine tasks), and challenges (e.g., job insecurity, trustworthiness). The review theorizes that AI will complement human labor, leading to task reallocation and changes in organizational structure, including a potential reduction in junior analyst roles.

As Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) is encroaching further on our work and social environments, we examine the increasingly common situation where teams of white-collar workers include both humans and AI.
We explore three key themes via a comprehensive (n=122) systematic literature review: key success factors, use cases for human-AI teams, and challenges faced when implementing human-Al teams.

paper_metadata.title: A Task-Based Approach to Generative AI: Evidence from a Field Experiment in Central Banking
paper_metadata.sample: 101 employees at the National Bank of Slovakia

productivity.finding: Generative AI increased task quality by 39-48% and reduced completion time by 23% for central bank employees, with effects more than twice as large for specialized tasks than for generalist tasks.

inequality.finding: Generative AI reduces performance inequality for generalist tasks by helping lower-skilled workers more on quality, but this equalizing effect does not apply to efficiency or to specialized tasks.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI strongly complements non-routine tasks, increasing performance on them by 58% compared to a 24% increase for routine tasks.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with central bank employees, generative AI increased task quality by 39-48% and reduced completion time by 23%, with larger gains for non-routine tasks and for lower-skilled workers on generalist tasks.
result.qual_notes: This field experiment at the National Bank of Slovakia (N=101) randomly assigned access to GPT-4o for a series of generalist and specialist tasks. The study finds a strong complementarity between AI and non-routine tasks, where performance gains were more than double those for routine tasks (58% vs 24%). While lower-skilled workers benefited more in terms of quality on generalist tasks, this equalizing effect did not extend to efficiency or specialist tasks. A simulation suggests that reallocating workers to tasks based on their AI-augmented comparative advantage could increase firm output by a further 7.3%.

evidence.snippet: This represents an average improvement of 48 percent (= 2.615/5.401) relative to when participants did not have access to generative AI.

Participants who had access to generative AI in the first seven tasks scored 2.3 points higher than those who did not have access in the first seven tasks, representing an average increase of 39 percent relative to the control mean.
Participants had on average 30 minutes to complete the tasks, so this represents an efficiency of improvement of about 23 percent relative to the control mean.

Our specifications indicate generative AI increased performance in generalist tasks: our within-subjects design estimates a treatment effect of 50 percent (= 2.710/5.401) while our between-subjects design estimates an effect of 36 percent (= 2.318/6.421) relative to the control means.
Relative to the control mean, generative AI increased performance in specialized tasks by between 108 and 117 percent, slightly more than twice the impact on generalist tasks.
For routine tasks, workers scored, on average, 24 percent higher when they had access to generative AI than when they did not.

For non-routine tasks, access to generative AI increased productivity by 58 percent across the sample, or 34 percent higher than the impact for routine tasks.

Results from a simulation exercise suggest that the organization can increase output by 7.3% through changes in how workers are allocated to tasks in the presence of generative AI.

We find empirical evidence of a complementarity between generative AI and non-routine work. Comparing sample averages, we find that access to generative AI increases performance by 24 percent on the set of routine tasks, versus 58 percent for non-routine tasks.

paper_metadata.title: ChatGPT decreases idea diversity in brainstorming
paper_metadata.sample: Participants in five creative challenge experiments from Lee and Chung (2024)

productivity.finding: While generative AI may enhance the creativity of individual ideas, it significantly reduces the diversity of the overall set of ideas in brainstorming tasks compared to human or web-search-aided approaches.

substitute_complement.finding: In a task where AI fully substituted for human idea generation, the diversity of ideas dropped to nearly zero (6% unique ideas) compared to 100% unique ideas from humans.

findings.experiment: Experiment 1: web search versus ChatGPT ideas
findings.metric_definition: The ratio of unique concepts to total ideas, with uniqueness based on a cosine similarity threshold of 0.8. Control group is web-search-assisted.

findings.experiment: Experiment 2a: web search versus human versus ChatGPT ideas
findings.metric_definition: The ratio of unique concepts to total ideas, with uniqueness based on a cosine similarity threshold of 0.8. Control group is human-only.
findings.note: The web-search-assisted control group had a unique fraction of 0.91.

findings.experiment: Experiment 2b: human versus ChatGPT-human versus ChatGPT-only ideas
findings.metric_definition: The ratio of unique concepts to total ideas, with uniqueness based on a cosine similarity threshold of 0.8. Control group is human-only. AI condition is ChatGPT-only (substitution).

findings.experiment: Experiment 3: web search versus ChatGPT ideas
findings.metric_definition: The ratio of unique concepts to total ideas, with uniqueness based on a cosine similarity threshold of 0.8. Control group is web-search-assisted.

findings.experiment: Experiment 5: chatbot empathy versus Google empathy (base and high)
findings.metric_definition: The ratio of unique concepts to total ideas, with uniqueness based on a cosine similarity threshold of 0.8. Comparison is between base empathy conditions. Control group is web search.

result.one_line: Using ChatGPT for brainstorming, while potentially improving individual ideas, significantly reduces the overall diversity of the generated idea pool compared to human-only or web-search-assisted methods.
result.qual_notes: This paper re-analyzes data from five brainstorming experiments originally conducted by Lee and Chung (2024). The authors measure idea diversity by calculating the fraction of 'unique' ideas in a set, based on the semantic similarity of their vector embeddings. They consistently find that idea sets generated with ChatGPT's help are less diverse than those from human-only or web-search-aided conditions. In an extreme case where AI fully substituted for human ideation, idea diversity collapsed, with only 6% of ideas being unique compared to 100% in the human-only group.

paper_metadata.title: Generative AI in Action: Field Experimental Evidence from Alibaba's Customer Service Operations
paper_metadata.sample: 5,940 new customer service agents (tenure less than one year) at Alibaba in China.

productivity.finding: A generative AI assistant significantly improved customer service agents' speed by reducing issue identification time and chat duration, and improved subjective service quality, but had no effect on objective quality.

inequality.finding: The AI assistant narrowed performance gaps, disproportionately benefiting low-performing agents while high-performing agents saw little speed improvement and a decline in service quality.

entry_level.finding: For new customer service agents with less than one year of tenure, a generative AI assistant improved performance, particularly for the lowest-skilled among them.

substitute_complement.finding: The AI assistant complemented human agents by automating diagnosis and solution proposals, enabling them to respond faster and produce more informative messages, rather than substituting for their communication efforts.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with customer service agents, a generative AI assistant improved performance by increasing speed and subjective quality, with the largest gains for low-skilled workers, while high-skilled workers' quality declined due to increased multitasking.
result.qual_notes: This large-scale field experiment at Alibaba randomly assigned 5,940 new customer service agents to receive access to a generative AI assistant. paper_metadata.title: The GenAI Wall Effect: Examining the Limits to Horizontal Expertise Transfer Between Occupational Insiders and Outsiders
paper_metadata.sample: 78 employees (12 web analysts, 26 marketing specialists, 40 technology specialists) at a UK-based global trading company

productivity.finding: Generative AI significantly improves the quality and speed of marketing content creation, but the magnitude of the benefit depends on the task's structure and the worker's domain knowledge.

inequality.finding: Generative AI compresses performance gaps between experts and non-experts, but its effectiveness is limited by a 'GenAI wall,' where workers with distant expertise fail to catch up on complex execution tasks.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI acts as a substitute for task-specific expertise, enabling workers from adjacent occupations to perform specialized tasks at a quality level comparable to insiders, challenging traditional divisions of labor.

result.one_line: Generative AI boosts productivity and compresses skill gaps, but its effectiveness is limited by a 'GenAI wall,' where workers with distant expertise (e.g., technologists writing marketing copy) fail to catch up to experts on complex execution tasks.
result.qual_notes: In a field experiment with 78 employees at a UK firm, participants from three groups (insiders, adjacent outsiders, distant outsiders) wrote marketing articles with and without a bespoke GenAI tool. The study distinguishes between structured 'conceptualization' tasks, where AI helped everyone improve, and unstructured 'execution' tasks, where a 'GenAI wall' appeared. This wall prevented workers with low domain knowledge (distant outsiders) from effectively using AI to close the quality gap with experts, unlike workers from adjacent fields who could.

paper_metadata.sample: Study 1: 1,988 American adults from Prolific; Study 2: 214 participants (students and community) from Warsaw University.

productivity.finding: In a competitive team setting, humans paired with an AI teammate showed a 26% performance decline compared to those paired with a human teammate, a deficit attributed to reduced commitment rather than mistrust in the AI.

substitute_complement.finding: AI serves as a less effective teammate than a human under competitive pressure, leading to significant human underperformance due to a 'commitment deficit' rather than issues of trust or coordination.

result.one_line: In a competitive lab experiment, humans teamed with an AI performed 26% worse than those teamed with a human, a deficit attributed to a motivational 'commitment deficit' rather than mistrust of the AI.
result.qual_notes: This paper presents two experiments (N=1,988 online, N=214 lab) on human-AI teaming. Study 1 found a significant performance drop when humans were teamed with AI under competitive pressure, but not in individual competition. This deficit was not explained by trust, as delegation rates to AI and human teammates were similar. Study 2 removed the competitive pressure and found no performance difference between human-AI and human-human teams, suggesting competition is a key trigger for the motivational commitment deficit.

result.one_line: In European firms, AI adoption increases labor productivity by 4.1% without reducing employment, with benefits concentrated in larger firms and those making complementary investments in software and training.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a quasi-experimental design, instrumenting AI adoption in European firms with adoption rates of matched US peers to establish causality. The analysis finds that productivity gains stem from capital deepening (augmenting labor) rather than labor substitution, as there is no significant effect on firm-level employment and a positive effect on average wages. However, the benefits are uneven, with only medium and large firms showing significant productivity increases, suggesting a risk of widening inequality between firms.

paper_metadata.title: Generative AI and the Productivity Divide: Human-AI Complementarities in Education and Knowledge Work (Working Paper)
paper_metadata.sample: 179 university students (mostly engineering majors) at Texas A&M University

productivity.finding: Access to generative AI increased task performance (exam scores) by approximately 17% compared to using traditional learning resources.

inequality.finding: Generative AI compresses inequality based on traditional skills like GPA but creates a new performance divide based on 'AI Interaction Competence' (AIC), with high-AIC workers benefiting disproportionately.

entry_level.finding: For early-career workers, the crucial skill for leveraging AI is not traditional domain knowledge but 'AI Interaction Competence'—the ability to effectively prompt, filter, and apply AI outputs.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI acts as a complement to and a multiplier of human competence, particularly for those with high AI Interaction Competence (AIC), rather than a simple substitute for labor.

findings.metric_definition: The percentage increase in normalized post-intervention exam scores for participants with LLM access compared to the baseline group.
findings.note: The paper reports this as an 'approximate 17% productivity lift'. The underlying means were 0.56 for the LLM group and 0.48 for the baseline group.

findings.metric_definition: Normalized exam score (0-1 scale) for novice participants who received a conceptual roadmap (scaffolding) while using an LLM.
findings.note: This group is compared to unguided novices, who scored 0.38.

findings.metric_definition: Normalized exam score (0-1 scale) for novice participants in the baseline LLM group (no scaffolding).
findings.note: This group is the baseline for scaffolded novices, who scored 0.45.

findings.comparison: Novices with LLM access vs. baseline
findings.metric_definition: The percentage increase in the standard deviation of exam scores among novice users with LLM access, indicating higher performance inequality.
findings.note: The paper states this 'more than doubling the variance'.

findings.metric: Standard deviation of performance (novices with LLM)
findings.comparison: Scaffolding vs. no scaffolding
findings.metric_definition: The percentage reduction in the standard deviation of exam scores among novice LLM users who received a scaffolding intervention (conceptual map).

result.one_line: Generative AI increases average performance in a knowledge acquisition task by ~17%, but widens the performance gap, with benefits accruing disproportionately to workers with high 'AI Interaction Competence' (AIC).
result.qual_notes: In a lab experiment with 179 students learning a technical topic, access to an LLM improved average exam scores by 17% compared to traditional resources. However, this gain was unevenly distributed: performance inequality among novices doubled, driven not by prior knowledge or GPA, but by a new skill called AI Interaction Competence (AIC). A simple 'scaffolding' intervention (providing a conceptual map) significantly reduced this performance gap for low-AIC users without hurting average performance.

evidence.snippet: GenAI access translated into an approximate 17% productivity lift consistent with field evidence of AI-assisted performance gains in professional work settings.
The treatment coefficient remained positive and statistically significant (p < 0.05).

The average post-intervention exam score among scaffolded novices was M = .45, SD = .14, compared to M = .38, SD = .22 in the baseline LLM group, with a weakly significant difference (p > .05, p < .10).
However, dispersion among novices increased by roughly 47% in standard deviation- -more than doubling the variance.

Notably, a scaffolding intervention (conceptual roadmaps and sequencing) reduced this dispersion by nearly 40% without lowering the mean, demonstrating that small process changes can stabilize outcomes.

productivity.finding: AI adoption is positively and significantly associated with better firm performance, with AI-using firms being 3.2 percentage points more likely to report high current performance and 6.4 percentage points more likely to report a sales increase.

substitute_complement.finding: AI primarily complements labor by augmenting existing tasks (reported by 44% of AI-using firms), while task substitution is less common (10%), and net employment changes are rare, with only 2% of firms reporting AI-driven employment decreases.

result.one_line: Based on a large survey of US firms, AI adoption is associated with better firm performance and is currently used more for task augmentation than substitution, with minimal net employment changes.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a large, nationally representative survey of over 117,000 U.S. firms (the BTOS) to analyze AI diffusion. The authors find that AI adoption is positively correlated with firm performance and sales growth. AI is primarily used to augment existing tasks (by 44% of AI-using firms) rather than substitute for them (10%), and direct employment impacts are rare, with only 2% of firms reporting AI-driven job cuts. The analysis distinguishes between firm-level functional deployment, worker-level task use, and operational investments, finding that functional deployment and investment are associated with employment declines, while worker-level use is not.

result.qual_notes: This study uses a staggered difference-in-differences design on a large proprietary dataset of 114,634 software engineers at 518 firms. result.qual_notes: This observational study uses an event-study design on US Current Population Survey (CPS) data, defining the treatment as the release of ChatGPT in November 2022. result.qual_notes: This online experiment with 1,174 adults in Argentina randomized access to a GPT-4.1 assistant for a business problem-solving task. The study finds that AI acts as a substitute for skills that are scarcer in low-education workers, such as abstract reasoning and writing. A follow-up task without AI showed that some gains persist, particularly for low-education workers who engaged deeply with the task, suggesting the effect is not purely delegation. result.one_line: In a field experiment with 758 management consultants, access to GPT-4 increased productivity on tasks within the AI's capabilities (completing 12.2% more tasks 25.1% faster with higher quality) but decreased performance on a task outside its capabilities by 19 percentage points, with lower-skilled workers benefiting the most.
result.qual_notes: This study was a preregistered, randomized field experiment with 758 management consultants at Boston Consulting Group (BCG). Participants were randomly assigned to a no-AI control or one of two GPT-4 treatment arms (with or without a prompt engineering overview) and completed realistic consulting tasks. The paper introduces the concept of a 'jagged technological frontier,' where AI improves performance on tasks it is suited for but degrades it on tasks it is not, even when tasks seem similar to humans. The study finds that AI acts as a skill-leveler, with lower-performing consultants seeing larger quality gains than higher-performing ones.

result.one_line: In a series of field experiments at a large online retailer, generative AI increased sales by up to 16.3% by improving customer service and product matching, with less experienced consumers benefiting the most.
result.qual_notes: This study conducted seven large-scale randomized field experiments on a global e-commerce platform to measure the impact of GenAI on sales across different business workflows, including customer service, search, and advertising. The effects on sales varied significantly, from a 16.3% increase for a pre-sale chatbot to a small negative effect for ad title generation, highlighting the importance of the baseline condition and model fine-tuning. The paper finds that GenAI disproportionately benefits less experienced consumers, thereby compressing performance gaps. It also provides evidence for strong complementarity between GenAI and human agents in customer service.

result.one_line: In a field experiment, mandating a structured collaborative protocol for using generative AI harmed team productivity, while training individuals to reframe AI as a 'thought partner' increased the likelihood of producing top-quality work.
result.qual_notes: This field experiment with 388 employees at a Fortune 500 retailer tested two interventions: a behavioral protocol for pairs (Task A) and cognitive reframing training for individuals (Task B). The behavioral protocol significantly reduced document quality and completion rates, likely due to high coordination costs. The cognitive training had a null effect on average quality due to ceiling effects, but a post-hoc analysis showed it increased the odds of producing a perfect-score document. Key limitations include a confound between treatment and time-of-day, high and differential attrition, and potential bias in the LLM-based grading.

result.one_line: A lab experiment with students finds that while AI access has no average effect on learning outcomes, it benefits high-achieving women most and substitutes for traditional study effort by reducing the number of practice questions attempted.
result.qual_notes: This lab experiment (N=572 students) randomized access to a generative AI chatbot (ChatGPT) during a learning task on a novel subject (Esperanto). The study found no average effect on final exam scores, but this masks significant heterogeneity: high-GPA women benefited most from guided AI access, while low-GPA students were more likely to get very low scores. AI access also caused a behavioral shift, with students attempting 12-17% fewer practice questions, suggesting AI substitutes for conventional study activities. Exploratory analysis of user prompts suggests that delegative "copy-paste" use of AI is associated with worse learning outcomes.

result.one_line: Labor market outcomes for AI-exposed occupations and recent graduates worsened before the launch of ChatGPT, but education in LLM-relevant skills improved job prospects after its release.
result.qual_notes: This study analyzes three large-scale U.S. datasets: unemployment insurance records, LinkedIn profiles, and university syllabi. It finds that unemployment risk in AI-exposed jobs began rising in early 2022, and recent graduates (from 2021 onward) entered these jobs at lower rates, well before ChatGPT's launch. This challenges the narrative that generative AI is the primary driver of recent labor market deterioration in these fields. However, after ChatGPT, graduates with more education in LLM-relevant skills had higher pay and shorter job searches, suggesting these skills are complementary to new AI tools.

result.one_line: Using résumé and job posting data from U.S. firms, this study finds that GenAI adoption leads to a significant decline in junior employment, particularly in AI-exposed occupations, driven primarily by reduced hiring rather than increased separations.

The authors find that the decline in junior employment is concentrated in high-exposure occupations and is driven by a sharp drop in hiring, while senior employment remains unaffected. Task-level analysis suggests this is due to GenAI substituting for tasks typically performed by junior workers, with no evidence of juniors being reallocated to more senior tasks.

paper_metadata.title: Looking for the Ladder: Is AI Impacting Entry-Level Jobs?
paper_metadata.sample: Over 238 million US job postings from September 2019 to August 2025, via the Lightcast dataset.

inequality.finding: Job postings for junior and senior roles in AI-exposed occupations have declined in parallel, suggesting the downturn is not disproportionately affecting junior workers relative to senior ones.

entry_level.finding: The recent decline in employment for early-career workers in AI-exposed occupations is better explained by macroeconomic shocks, like monetary policy tightening, rather than displacement by generative AI.

substitute_complement.finding: The argument for rapid, large-scale substitution of entry-level workers by AI is implausible, as the downturn in hiring began months before key generative AI tools were released and widely adopted by firms.

result.one_line: The recent decline in entry-level jobs in AI-exposed fields is attributable to macroeconomic factors like interest rate hikes, not displacement by generative AI, as the hiring slowdown predates ChatGPT's release.
result.qual_notes: This paper critiques the narrative that generative AI is displacing entry-level workers by analyzing US job posting data. The authors demonstrate that the hiring downturn in AI-exposed occupations began in Spring 2022, six months before ChatGPT's launch, and coincided with the start of aggressive interest rate hikes. They also find that job postings for junior and senior roles in these occupations declined in parallel, suggesting a broad-based shock rather than targeted AI-driven substitution. The paper concludes that current data does not support the AI displacement hypothesis for young workers.

Lending significant academic weight to this narrative is the working paper by Erik Brynjolfsson, Bharat Chandar, and Ruyu Chen (2025), which is aptly titled "Canaries in the Coal Mine? Six Facts about the Recent Employment Effects of Artificial Intelligence."¹ Its headline finding is striking: a 16 percent relative decline in employment for early-career workers (ages 22–25) in the most AI-exposed occupations since the widespread release of generative AI in November 2022.

The most plausible explanation is that the data patterns observed are not early warnings of large-scale technological displacement, but rather the predictable consequences of a classic macroeconomic shock: the sharpest monetary policy tightening cycle in four decades.

There is no evidence that job postings for junior roles within occupations most exposed to AI have declined more than postings for senior positions. Postings for both levels of seniority have been falling roughly in parallel since their peak in Spring 2022, with the decline in junior positions stabilizing faster.
The central weakness of the hypothesis that AI is the main driver of the recent entry-level downturn is its implausible timeline.

This chart illustrates that job postings for the most AI-exposed quintile (Q5, dark blue line) peaked in Spring 2022 and began a steep decline well before the November 2022 launch of ChatGPT, indicating a pre-existing shock.
The strong inverse relationship, with postings declining as interest rates rise from March 2022 onward, points to monetary policy as a primary driver.

result.one_line: In a large field experiment with a recruitment firm, using a generative AI voice agent to conduct interviews for entry-level jobs increased job offers by 12% and one-month retention by 18% without harming the productivity of hired workers.
result.qual_notes: This large-scale field experiment (N=70,884) randomly assigned job applicants in the Philippines to be interviewed by either a human or an AI voice agent for entry-level customer service roles. The study finds that AI-led interviews, by being more structured and consistent, collected more hiring-relevant information, leading to better match quality. A secondary finding is that lower-skilled applicants were more likely to choose an AI interviewer when given the option.

result.one_line: In a field experiment creating marketing ads, human-AI teams were 50% more productive per worker than human-human teams, producing higher quality text but lower quality images, and shifting collaboration from peer-to-peer interaction to a delegation workflow.
result.qual_notes: This study was a large-scale randomized experiment (N=2,234) where participants were assigned to either human-human or human-AI teams to create marketing ads on a custom platform. The AI was an agentic collaborator capable of most human actions. result.one_line: The recent decline in early-career hiring is robustly explained by the shift to remote work, while the widely-blamed effect of generative AI disappears when controlling for this confound.
result.qual_notes: Using two large datasets of new hires and job postings from four countries, this study employs a difference-in-differences design to disentangle the effects of GenAI and Work-From-Home (WFH) exposure on junior hiring. The results suggest that studies attributing the decline in junior hiring to GenAI may suffer from omitted variable bias, as WFH is the more robust predictor.

result.qual_notes: The study uses a difference-in-differences design on U.S. job postings from 2018-2025, comparing high- vs. low-substitution occupations after November 2022 while controlling for GenAI exposure to isolate the substitution effect. Labor Demand in the Shadow of Generative AI: Evidence from the U.S. Job Posting Data

This paper estimates the causal impact of generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) on labor demand in the United States. Exploiting the release of ChatGPT in November 2022 as an exogenous shock, we apply difference-in-differences and event study designs to 285 million online job postings from Lightcast during 2018Q1-2025Q2.
We further show that GenAI-driven displacement is not confined to the U.S.: parallel effects emerge in the U.K. and Germany, where macroeconomic conditions and labor market institutions differ.

result.one_line: In a randomized trial with 137 law students, access to advanced generative AI tools (a reasoning model and a RAG-based system) increased productivity by 50-130% on complex legal tasks by improving work quality and reducing completion time.

result.one_line: Using comprehensive administrative data, this study finds that the release of ChatGPT was followed by a sharp, persistent 12-13% decline in hiring and employment for early-career workers in the most AI-exposed US industries.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a difference-in-differences event study design on U.S. Quarterly Workforce Indicators (QWI) data to analyze labor market outcomes for early-career workers (ages 22-24) following ChatGPT's release. The primary finding is a large, immediate, and persistent decline in hiring, which fully explains a 12.4% regression-adjusted drop in employment in the most AI-exposed industries. A triple-difference analysis comparing early-career to prime-age workers suggests some of this trend began before ChatGPT, potentially linked to remote work, but that a discontinuous drop in hiring for new and replacement roles occurred right after its release. The paper finds little evidence that monetary policy shocks can explain this sharp decline.

evidence.snippet: This method implies a 12.4% decline in employment from the release of ChatGPT to 2025q2, which is a bit smaller in magnitude than that suggested by Brynjolfsson et al., and also a bit smaller than the unadjusted 15% decline implied by the unadjusted tabulation.
This decline in hires (top left panel) has not recovered over time; hires over the last four quarters of available data are 12.7% below the corresponding reference quarters on average.
It shows that early career employment in the most AI-exposed quintile of employer declined by 15.2% from the reference period of 2022q4 to 2025q2.
The 9.1% immediate reduction in job gains in the first four post-ChatGPT quarters is similar in magnitude to the decline in overall hires over the same period.
However, in more recent quarters, replacement hiring has declined a bit more than hiring overall; the last four available quarter suggest that that replacement hires have declined by about 15%.
As of 2025q1, separations have declined 11.5% in comparison with the four quarter reference.

paper_metadata.title: Agentic AI and Human-in-the-Loop Interventions: Field Experimental Evidence from Alibaba's Customer Service Operations
paper_metadata.sample: 647 customer service workers and 680,676 service chats at Alibaba's Taobao platform

productivity.finding: Agentic AI deployment in customer service reduces average chat duration by 3.2% overall, with a 16.8% reduction for AI-eligible chats, but it also significantly lowers customer ratings for those AI-handled chats.

substitute_complement.finding: Agentic AI substitutes for human labor on standardized tasks, allowing workers to reallocate their attention and focus more effectively on complex, AI-ineligible tasks.

findings.note: The paper reports a coefficient of -0.032 for ln(Chat Duration), which is interpreted as a 3.2% decline.

findings.note: The paper reports a coefficient of -0.168 for ln(Chat Duration), which is interpreted as a 16.8% decline.

findings.metric_definition: Change in customer rating on a five-point scale for AI-eligible chats.

findings.note: A negative coefficient indicates that treated workers devoted a smaller share of time to other chats, suggesting greater focus.

result.one_line: In a field experiment with customer service gig workers, an agentic AI increased service speed by 3.2% and improved quality on complex human-handled tasks, but decreased customer ratings on the simpler tasks it handled autonomously.
result.qual_notes: This randomized field experiment at Alibaba's Taobao platform involved 647 customer service workers. Treated workers supervised an agentic AI on 'AI-eligible' chats and continued to handle 'AI-ineligible' chats, while control workers handled all chats manually. The study finds that AI substitutes for labor on simple tasks, freeing workers to reallocate attention and perform better on complex tasks. However, the effectiveness of human intervention when the AI fails depends heavily on timing and the nature of the failure, with interventions in emotionally deteriorated chats being much less effective.

evidence.snippet: Table 2 shows that the deployment of agentic AI improves service speed at the aggregate level, with average chat duration declining by 3.2 percent (p < 0.001).
As shown in Table 3 Column (1), average chat duration declines by 16.8 percent in AI-eligible chats (p < 0.001).
For AI-eligible chats, Table 3 Column (3) shows that customer ratings decline by 0.412 points relative to those of control workers (p < 0.001).
Table 4 Column (1) shows that average chat duration also declines by 1.8 percent in AI-ineligible chats (p<0.05).
For AI-ineligible chats, by contrast, Table 4 Column (3) shows that treated workers receive customer ratings that are 0.091 points higher than those of control workers (p < 0.01).

paper_metadata.title: The Impact of Large Language Models on Open-Source Innovation: Evidence from GitHub Copilot
paper_metadata.sample: 1,187 matched pairs of Python and R open-source packages on GitHub, encompassing over 620,000 commits.

productivity.finding: GitHub Copilot increases the volume of open-source software contributions by 28-40%, with the effect being larger for incremental than for substantive contributions.

substitute_complement.finding: Generative AI complements open-source contributors by increasing their output, but it disproportionately boosts incremental contributions (maintenance, refactoring) over substantive ones (new features), shifting effort toward exploitation.

result.one_line: In a natural experiment on GitHub, access to Copilot increased open-source developer contributions by 28-40%, but disproportionately boosted incremental maintenance tasks over the creation of new, substantive features.
result.qual_notes: This study uses a difference-in-differences design comparing Python (supported by Copilot) and R (unsupported) open-source packages. It finds that AI's productivity boost is larger for tasks with more existing context (incremental changes, high-activity projects) and that this gap widens as the AI model improves. The findings suggest AI may shift innovation from exploration (new features) toward exploitation (refining existing codebases).

paper_metadata.title: Firm Data on AI
paper_metadata.sample: Nearly 6,000 senior business executives at US, UK, German, and Australian firms, plus a survey of US employees.

productivity.finding: Executives report that AI has had a small positive impact on firm productivity over the past three years (+0.29%) but expect a much larger boost over the next three years (+1.4%).

entry_level.finding: Among UK firms expecting AI to reduce employment, around two-thirds of the reduction is expected to come from hiring fewer new employees rather than from increased exits.

substitute_complement.finding: Executives expect AI to substitute for labor, predicting an average firm-level employment reduction of 0.7% over the next three years, in stark contrast to employees who expect a 0.5% increase.

result.one_line: Based on a survey of nearly 6,000 executives, AI is expected to boost firm productivity by 1.4% and reduce employment by 0.7% over the next three years, a sharp increase from the negligible impacts reported for the past three years.
result.qual_notes: This study surveys senior executives at nearly 6,000 firms in the US, UK, Germany, and Australia about their firms' past and expected impacts of AI. While past impacts on productivity (+0.29%) and employment (0.00%) were minimal, executives anticipate significant future effects. The expected employment reduction is predicted to come primarily from reduced hiring rather than layoffs. Notably, employees' expectations diverge from executives', with employees predicting a 0.5% increase in employment due to AI.
